annotations.text: The most plausible unifying diagnosis is a metabolic encephalopathy secondary to manganese toxicity, likely secondary to chronic liver dysfunction from an occult cause, or possibly due to a genetic disorder of metal metabolism.
Another possibility is a disorder of copper metabolism, such as Wilson disease, which can present with basal ganglia lesions, psychiatric symptoms, and cognitive decline.
The most compelling and likely diagnosis, given the totality of the clinical and radiologic findings, is **Wilson disease with late-onset, atypical presentation**.

the patient’s chronic anemia raises the possibility of iron metabolism disorders, which may have downstream effects on manganese homeostasis, as both metals share transport pathways via divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1) and ferroportin.
The chronic anemia may reflect hemolysis from copper toxicity.
If these were low ceruloplasmin and elevated urinary copper, Wilson disease would be confirmed.

The symmetric basal ganglia hypointensity on susceptibility MRI, the progressive cognitive and behavioral decline over two years, the absence of infectious or neoplastic etiologies, the lack of response to antibiotics, and the normal liver function tests (which can be misleading in early or compensated Wilson disease) all point toward this diagnosis.

This pattern—slowly progressive cognitive decline with behavioral disinhibition, executive dysfunction, and visuomotor deterioration—is characteristic of neurodegenerative dementias, particularly frontotemporal dementia (FTD) variants, such as the behavioral variant (bvFTD) or corticobasal syndrome (CBS)
